summary: Q1 2026 DeFi losses: $137M across 15 incidents. Top hacks: Step Finance ($27.3M), Truebit ($26.2M), Resolv ($25M+ via compromised AWS KMS key), SwapNet ($13.4M). Social engineering has replaced code exploits as dominant attack vector.

Resolv hack (March 22) is most relevant to LEVER: compromised private key (AWS KMS) allowed attacker to mint 80M USR tokens and drain $25M+. This is an infrastructure/key management attack, not a code bug. LEVER's keeper and oracle bot keys are the equivalent risk surface.

Oracle manipulation ranked #2 attack vector in 2024: $52M across 37 incidents. Flash loan price distortion, compromised data sources, TWAP exploitation. LEVER's OracleAdapter is the critical security surface. Multi-source aggregation, circuit breakers, and TWAP are recommended defenses (LEVER already uses smoothing).

Access control vulnerabilities remain #1 cause of financial losses: $953.2M in 2024. LEVER uses OpenZeppelin AccessControl with role separation (ADMIN, KEEPER, ORACLE, MARKET_MANAGER), which is the correct pattern.

AI models can now exploit 72% of smart contract vulnerabilities autonomously (GPT-5.3-Codex benchmark). This raises the bar for audit thoroughness. AI-assisted auditing tools should be part of LEVER's pre-mainnet security process.

entities.role: Exploited 72% of smart contract vulnerabilities in benchmark, fixed 41.5%

relationships.relationship: analogous risk: compromised infrastructure keys (keeper/oracle bots) could enable similar attack on LEVER
OracleAdapter is LEVER's #1 security surface; uses smoothing but should verify multi-source and circuit breaker coverage
72% autonomous exploit rate means AI-assisted auditing is now essential, not optional

criticalRisks: Key management: Keeper and oracle bot private keys are the Resolv-equivalent attack surface. Must use hardware security modules or multi-sig, NOT raw AWS KMS alone.
Oracle manipulation: OracleAdapter's smoothing algorithm is a defense, but flash loan attacks and compromised Polymarket data could feed false PI values. Verify circuit breaker thresholds.
Access control: OpenZeppelin AccessControl is correct but must verify no functions are unprotected. ADMIN role key management is critical.
Reentrancy: LeverVault (ERC-4626) deposit/withdrawal flows and ExecutionEngine position open/close flows must be reentrancy-guarded.
AI-assisted attacks: 72% autonomous exploit rate means LEVER contracts will be targeted by AI tools. Pre-mainnet audit should include AI-assisted fuzzing.
recommendedActions: SECURE workstream should audit key management for all bot/keeper/oracle infrastructure
Add circuit breaker to OracleAdapter if not present (halt on >X% price change in single update)
Verify all external-facing functions have proper access control modifiers
Run AI-assisted fuzzing (GPT-5.3-Codex or equivalent) against all contracts before mainnet
Establish incident response playbook (the '60-minute playbook' pattern from dev.to article)

workstreamTags.SECURE: CRITICAL: Q1 2026 shows $137M lost. Resolv hack ($25M via key compromise) is directly relevant to LEVER's keeper/oracle bot infrastructure. Oracle manipulation is #2 attack vector. AI can now exploit 72% of vulnerabilities autonomously. Pre-mainnet security review must cover: key management, oracle circuit breakers, access control completeness, reentrancy, and AI-assisted fuzzing.
workstreamTags.BUILD: Verify OracleAdapter has circuit breaker for abnormal price movements. Verify all keeper/oracle bot keys use HSM or multi-sig. Run AI-assisted fuzzing before mainnet.
workstreamTags.ADVISOR: $137M in Q1 2026 DeFi losses is context for why LEVER needs security as a differentiator. Investors will ask about audit status.
workstreamTags.CEO: Security narrative for investors: LEVER uses immutable deployment (no proxy upgrade attacks), OpenZeppelin AccessControl, oracle smoothing, and plans for pre-mainnet AI-assisted audit. Differentiate from protocols that got hacked by cutting corners.
